label: Pop Hook-First — AABB Couplets
blurb: The HERMES default: hook-forward pop structure, classic end-rhymed couplets (lines 1&2 rhyme, 3&4 rhyme).
sourceNote: Baseline combinator behavior — the original engine shape, kept as the default pack.

label: AABA / 32-Bar Classic
blurb: A longer arrangement that rides out on a repeated final hook instead of a single closing one — in the spirit of the AABA convention: cycle through once, then return without new lyrical material.
sourceNote: AABA production rule (A-A-B-A, ‘once the first cycle is complete there tend not to be any new lyrics’) — Open Music Theory / Summach, MTO 17.3.

label: Verse-Chorus — Alternating (ABAB)
blurb: Verse-led arrangement with an alternating rhyme scheme (lines 1&3 rhyme, 2&4 rhyme) instead of sequential couplets — a different craft feel over the same verse-chorus shape.
sourceNote: ABAB is a standard poetic rhyme-scheme pattern, offered here as a craft-variety dial (genre-to-scheme frequency claims did not survive this repo's research verification — see docs/pattern-packs.md).

label: Ballad — Common Meter (XAXA)
blurb: Only the even-numbered lines rhyme (2nd and 4th) — the classic ballad/hymn rhyme convention, leaving lines 1 and 3 free.
sourceNote: XAXA is the standard ballad/common-meter rhyme convention (see Wikipedia: Common meter; LitCharts: Common Meter).

label: Monorhyme Chant (AAAA)
blurb: Every line lands on the same rhyme family — a chant-like, hypnotic feel, paired with a short, punchy length.
sourceNote: Monorhyme is a standard poetic device (Wikipedia: Rhyme scheme); short-form keeps it tight rather than repetitive.

label: Enclosed Rhyme (ABBA)
blurb: Enclosed/mirrored rhyme (1st & 4th lines rhyme, 2nd & 3rd rhyme) inside a radio-length arrangement — a more intricate, folded rhyme feel.
sourceNote: ABBA (enclosed rhyme) is a standard poetic rhyme-scheme pattern (Wikipedia: Rhyme scheme).

label: Strophic, Verse-Led (AABB)
blurb: No intro, no bridge — a leaner, self-contained verse-forward shape closer to a strophic feel than a padded modern-pop arrangement.
sourceNote: Strophic form and its genre priors (blues, early hip-hop, 1950s–60s rock) — Open Music Theory / Summach. The engine's verse-first grammar approximates this shape; it is not a literal 12-bar harmonic transcription.
